title: PCA in the Real World
concept: PCA shows up across real applications: compressing images, visualizing high-dimensional data in 2D, and speeding up model training by shrinking huge feature sets.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's a common target when choosing how many components to keep?

options.text: Enough components to preserve around 95% of the cumulative variance
Exactly 1 component, no matter the dataset
As few components as possible, even if most variance is lost

exercises.explain: Right! Around 95% cumulative variance is a common target. Now let's see where PCA actually gets used.

cards.title: A blurry photo that's still recognizable
cards.body: Ever seen a heavily compressed image online that's a little blurry, but you can still instantly tell what it shows? That's dimensionality reduction at work on real pixels — and PCA is one of the classic tools behind it.

cards.title: PCA shows up everywhere
cards.body: PCA isn't just a classroom idea — it's used across the real world for tasks like compressing images and files, drawing 2D maps of impossibly complex data, and speeding up model training by shrinking huge feature sets down to the handful of directions that matter most.

cards.title: A subway map isn't geographically exact
cards.body: A subway map doesn't show every twist and turn of the actual tunnels underground — that would be a cluttered mess to read. It simplifies everything down to a flat page while still preserving what matters: which stations connect, and roughly how they're arranged. That's what PCA does when it draws high-dimensional data as a simple 2D scatter plot.

cards.title: Training a face-recognition model faster
cards.body: A face-recognition system originally tracks 10,000 pixel values per photo. Running PCA first compresses that down to around 150 principal components that still capture the essential facial variation, so the model trains on far fewer inputs in a fraction of the time — without a meaningful drop in accuracy.

cards.title: Compressed doesn't mean fake
cards.body: It's tempting to think PCA-compressed images or data are just "blurry, less accurate approximations" not worth using. In reality, for many tasks — like training a model or drawing a quick visualization — the small amount of detail lost is well worth the massive speed and storage gains, and the dropped detail was often mostly noise anyway.

cards.title: Full data vs. PCA-compressed data
cards.body: What changes when PCA compresses a dataset:

left.text:
Every original feature/pixel kept
Slow, heavy, hard to visualize

right.text:
Just the top components kept
Faster, lighter, easy to plot in 2D

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a real-world use of PCA?

options.text: Compressing an image's file size while keeping it visually recognizable
Sorting emails into spam vs. not spam
Finding three natural groups of shoppers based on buying habits

exercises.explain: Right! Compressing images while preserving their recognizable structure is a classic PCA use case.

exercises.prompt: True or false: PCA can help visualize data with hundreds of features by compressing it down to just 2 dimensions for a scatter plot.
exercises.explain: True! Reducing to the top 2 principal components is a common way to visualize high-dimensional data.

exercises.sentence: Reducing thousands of pixel values down to a small set of principal components can make a model ___ to train, since it processes far fewer inputs.
exercises.explain: Fewer inputs to process means faster training — one of PCA's most common practical benefits.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario matches a real-world PCA use case?

options.text: A photo app shrinks a 10-megapixel image's data down to the components that preserve its visible detail, cutting the file size dramatically
A photo app sorts pictures into folders labeled "cats" and "dogs"
A photo app groups visually similar photos into albums automatically

exercises.explain: Correct! Shrinking image data down to its most informative components while preserving visible detail is exactly what PCA-based compression does.

exercises.prompt: A research lab has patient data with 500 medical measurements per patient and wants to (1) plot patients on a single 2D chart to spot patterns visually, and (2) train a diagnosis model faster. A teammate suggests skipping PCA — just picking 2 measurements at random to plot, and feeding all 500 raw measurements into the model. What's the problem with that plan?

options.text: Random measurements likely miss most of the real variation between patients, and training on all 500 raw features is slower and noisier than training on a handful of PCA components that preserve most of the meaningful signal
There's no problem — random measurements always work as well as PCA
PCA can only be used for images, not medical data
Using more raw features always makes a model faster to train

exercises.explain: Exactly! PCA finds the directions that actually capture the most meaningful variation for both visualizing and training — random picks and raw, unreduced features waste the visualization and slow down training.
